nativeRange.habitat: Small to medium-sized clear-water creeks and slow marginal zones with abundant aquatic vegetation; found in the lower Ucayali and Marañón river systems

decorPreferences: broad-leaved plants (Anubias, Java fern) for grazing and resting
driftwood and smooth rocks for aufwuchs colonisation
gentle flow — sponge or HOB filter with spray bar
soft substrate to prevent ventral abrasion

diet.notes: Specialised aufwuchs grazer: feeds almost exclusively on algae, diatoms, and the biofilm (microorganisms, bacteria, microalgae) that colonises surfaces in mature aquaria. Supplement with sinking algae wafers, spirulina wafers, and blanched vegetables (zucchini, cucumber, spinach). Unlike common Oto species, O. cocama rarely accepts protein-heavy foods. A continuously available grazing surface is essential — the species feeds in short, frequent bursts throughout the day and will decline quickly in algae-stripped or immature tanks.

goodWith: small peaceful characins (ember tetras, neon tetras, rummy-nose tetras)
avoidWith: large or aggressive fish that will outcompete for food or cause stress
fast-moving boisterous species that disturb biofilm colonies

summary: A strikingly patterned Peruvian dwarf suckermouth catfish (~4.4 cm) distinguished by bold vertical zebra bands rather than the dotted lateral stripe of common Otocinclus. Requires a mature, biofilm-rich planted tank; more sensitive and more expensive than typical Oto species.
careNotes: Otocinclus cocama is endemic to the lower Ucayali and Marañón river drainages of Peru and is the most visually distinctive member of the genus, bearing bold vertically elongated blotches spanning from the dorsal midline to the ventral flank — a stark contrast to the spotted or striped patterns of O. vestitus and O. macrospilus. Adult size is 3.5–4.4 cm SL. Maintain soft, slightly acidic to neutral water (pH 6.0–7.5, gH 2–12, temperature 21–25°C) and target high oxygen saturation with gentle filtration; turnover of 4–5× tank volume per hour via a sponge filter or spray-bar HOB is ideal. The species is an obligate aufwuchs grazer and must be introduced only into established aquaria with visible algae and biofilm colonising hard surfaces — do not add to tanks under three months old, as newly cycled tanks lack the biofilm layer these fish depend on. Keep in groups of at least 6; solitary specimens become stressed and waste away. Supplement grazing with sinking spirulina wafers, algae rounds, and blanched zucchini or cucumber placed on the substrate. Unlike common Oto species, O. cocama seldom competes well for food alongside faster, more assertive bottom dwellers. The Zebra Oto commands a notably higher retail price (often US $10–20+ per fish) than common Otocinclus species and is predominantly wild-caught; acclimate new arrivals extremely slowly (drip acclimation over 60+ minutes) as they are sensitive to abrupt parameter shifts. IUCN lists the species as Endangered (EN) owing to wild collection pressure — source captive-bred specimens where available.
breedingNotes: Rarely accomplished in captivity. Spawning has been documented but is uncommon in home aquaria. The male initiates courtship with characteristic T-position behaviour similar to corydoras. Adhesive eggs (clutches of 30–50) are deposited singly or in small clusters on broad plant leaves and hardscape. No parental care follows; parents may consume eggs if not removed. Eggs hatch in approximately 3 days at 24°C. Fry are extremely small and require green water, infusoria, or biofilm-coated surfaces as their first food — standard powdered fry foods are often ignored. High fry mortality is typical without a well-established algae culture.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Otocinclus cocama
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (40–45 mm SL), temperature (21–25°C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness (36–179 ppm ≈ gH 2–10), tank base dimensions (45×30 cm minimum), diet (aufwuchs, algae, biofilm), native range (lower Ucayali and Marañón, Peru), habitat (dense marginal vegetation, clear water), breeding behaviour, and compatibility guidance. Group minimum of 4–6 recommended.

additional.name: FishBase — Otocinclus cocama
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 4.4 cm SL), taxonomy (Loricariidae, Siluriformes), native range (Peru — lower Ucayali and Marañón), habitat (clear-water creeks with marginal vegetation), IUCN Endangered status, facultative air-breathing note, and high tooth count distinguishing this species. Temperature and pH data consistent with SeriouslyFish.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Otocinclus Catfish Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade and husbandry source. Confirmed temperature 21–26°C, pH 6.0–7.5, mature-tank requirement, aufwuchs-grazer diet (algae wafers, Repashy Soilent Green, blanched zucchini), group-keeping, and compatibility with dwarf shrimp and small community fish. Notes O. cocama as one of three commonly traded Otocinclus species.

additional.name: Aquariadise — Zebra Otocinclus Care Guide
additional.notes: Supplementary hobbyist source. Confirmed hardness up to 12 dGH, group size of 6–8, lifespan 3–5 years (conservative estimate used), ~$15 per fish retail price, IUCN Endangered status, predominantly wild-caught with high post-purchase mortality risk, and drip-acclimation recommendation.

fish.breedingNotes: Rare in captivity. T-position spawning on broad leaves; 30–50 adhesive eggs per clutch; hatch ~3 days at 24°C. Fry require green water or infusoria — standard powdered foods not accepted readily. High fry mortality without established algae culture.
